title: Your first library endpoint
instruction:
A library needs an API. Let's start with a welcome endpoint.

An HTTP handler is just a function that takes a request (`r`) and writes a response (`w`).

Run this — it creates a handler that writes "Welcome to the Library API!" to the response.
successMessage: That's a working HTTP handler for your library. In production you'd call `http.ListenAndServe(\":8080\", nil)` and it becomes a real API server.

title: Writing a search response
instruction:
`http.ResponseWriter` is an `io.Writer` — you can use `fmt.Fprintln`, `fmt.Fprintf`, or `w.Write()` to send a response.

You can also set the status code and headers before writing:
```
w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)       // 200
w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "text/plain")
```

Add a second handler `searchHandler` that sets the `Content-Type` to `text/plain` and responds with `"Searching for: [query]"` where `query` comes from the URL query parameter `q`. Then call it with q="Go programming".

codeChecks.message: Get the query parameter: r.URL.Query().Get(\"q\")
Write the response to w using fmt.Fprintf or fmt.Fprintln.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Query parameters are read with r.URL.Query().Get(\"q\"). Headers are set before writing the body. This is how your library search endpoint works.

title: Status codes — book availability
instruction:
HTTP status codes tell the client what happened:
- `200` — OK (book found)
- `201` — Created (new book added)
- `400` — Bad Request
- `404` — Not Found (book not in catalog)
- `500` — Internal Server Error

`w.WriteHeader(http.StatusNotFound)` — use the named constants, not magic numbers.

Run this. Two requests — one available book, one missing. Add the missing `404` case: if `isbn` is not `"978-0141036144"`, write a `404` status and `"book not found"` message.
expectedOutput: Status: 200 | Body: Found book: 1984 by George Orwell

codeChecks.message: Write the 404 status: w.WriteHeader(http.StatusNotFound)
Write \"book not found\" in the body.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 200 for available books, 404 for missing ones. Always use http.StatusXxx constants — they are self-documenting and less error-prone than raw numbers.

title: Responding with JSON — book catalog
instruction:
Most library APIs respond with JSON. The pattern:
1. Set `Content-Type: application/json`
2. Use `json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(value)` to write JSON directly to the response

Add a second book to the `catalog` map (for isbn `"978-0061120084"`), then handle it in `bookHandler` and return JSON.
hint: \"978-0061120084\": Book{ISBN: \"978-0061120084\", Title: \"To Kill a Mockingbird\", Author: \"Harper Lee\", Available: true}

codeChecks.message: Add a second book with key \"978-0061120084\" to the catalog map.
Replace the TODO comment with your book entry.

successMessage: JSON responses, status codes, 404 handling — this is a real library API pattern. json.NewEncoder(w).Encode writes directly to the response without allocating an extra buffer.

title: Reading a request body — check out a book
instruction:
For POST requests, the client sends data in the request body to check out a book.

Decode it with `json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&v)`.

Always close the body: `defer r.Body.Close()`

Run this. The handler reads a JSON body and echoes back the member's name and the book title being checked out.
hint: Change the body JSON from Alex to your own name.
starter:
package main

import (
	"encoding/json"
	"fmt"
	"net/http"
	"net/http/httptest"
	"strings"
)

type CheckoutRequest struct {
	MemberName string `json:"member_name"`
	BookTitle  string `json:"book_title"`
}

func checkoutHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	defer r.Body.Close()

	var req CheckoutRequest
	if err := json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&req); err != nil {
		w.WriteHeader(http.StatusBadRequest)
		fmt.Fprintln(w, `{"error":"invalid request body"}`)
		return
	}

	w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
	fmt.Fprintf(w, "Checked out '%s' for %s\
", req.BookTitle, req.MemberName)
}

func main() {
	body := `{"member_name":"Alex","book_title":"1984"}`
	req := httptest.NewRequest("POST", "/checkout", strings.NewReader(body))
	req.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	w := httptest.NewRecorder()
	checkoutHandler(w, req)
	fmt.Printf("Status: %d\
", w.Code)
	fmt.Print(w.Body.String())
}

codeChecks.message: Change the member_name in the body JSON to your own name.

successMessage: POST body decoded in two lines. json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&req) handles everything — parsing, type conversion, error reporting.

title: Multiple routes — library endpoints
instruction:
Register multiple routes with `http.HandleFunc`:

```
http.HandleFunc("/", welcomeHandler)
http.HandleFunc("/books", booksHandler)
http.HandleFunc("/health", healthHandler)
```

Add a third route `/members` that responds with `"List of library members"`.
hint: mux.HandleFunc(\"/members\", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { fmt.Fprintln(w, \"List of library members\") })

codeChecks.message: Register a /members route: mux.HandleFunc(\"/members\", ...)
The /members handler should respond with \"List of library members\".
Replace the TODO comment with your route.

successMessage: Three routes, three handlers. http.NewServeMux() is Go's built-in router. For simple library APIs it is all you need.

title: Method checking — GET vs POST for books
instruction:
REST APIs use different HTTP methods for different operations:
- `GET` — list books
- `POST` — add a new book
- `PUT` / `PATCH` — update a book
- `DELETE` — remove a book

Check the method with `r.Method`.

Add a `case http.MethodPost` that responds with `"Adding a new book..."` and status 201.
hint: case http.MethodPost:  w.WriteHeader(http.StatusCreated)  fmt.Fprintln(w, \"Adding a new book...\")

codeChecks.message: Add case http.MethodPost: to the switch.
Use w.WriteHeader(http.StatusCreated) for 201.
Respond with \"Adding a new book...\" for POST.
Replace the TODO comment with your POST case.

successMessage: GET lists books, POST adds books, DELETE gets 405. Method checking keeps your library API semantically correct.

title: Middleware — logging library requests
hint: func timingMiddleware(next http.HandlerFunc) http.HandlerFunc { return func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { next(w, r); fmt.Println(\"Response sent\") } }

codeChecks.message: Define a timingMiddleware function.
Print \"Response sent\" after calling next.
Wrap the handler with timingMiddleware.
Replace the TODO comments with your middleware.

successMessage: The middleware chain: logging → timing → handler → timing finishes. Middlewares are composable — stack as many as you need.

title: Making an HTTP GET request — fetch book data
instruction:
Go is not just a server — it is also a great HTTP client.

`http.Get(url)` makes a GET request and returns a response.

Always close the body: `defer resp.Body.Close()`

Read the body with `io.ReadAll(resp.Body)`.

Run this — it calls a public JSON API (Open Library) and prints the status and first 100 characters of the response.
successMessage: http.Get, check the error, defer close, read the body. Three steps for every HTTP client call. The same pattern works for any library API.

title: Making an HTTP POST request — add a book
instruction:
`http.Post` sends a POST with a body:

```
resp, err := http.Post(url, "application/json", strings.NewReader(body))
```

For more control (custom headers, timeouts), use `http.NewRequest` + `http.Client`.

Run this. It POSTs JSON to a test API. Change the payload book title to your favorite book.
hint: Change \"1984\" in the JSON payload to your favorite book title.
starter:
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"io"
	"net/http"
	"strings"
)

func main() {
	payload := `{"title":"1984","author":"George Orwell"}`
	resp, err := http.Post(
		"https://httpbin.org/post",
		"application/json",
		strings.NewReader(payload),
	)
	if err != nil {
		fmt.Println("Error:", err)
		return
	}
	defer resp.Body.Close()

	body, _ := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
	fmt.Println("Status:", resp.StatusCode)
	_ = body
	fmt.Println("Book added successfully")
}

codeChecks.message: Change the title in the payload to your favorite book.

successMessage: POST with a JSON body — the foundation of every API call you will make. http.Post for simple cases, http.NewRequest + http.Client when you need headers, timeouts, or retries.

title: Setting request headers — API key
instruction:
Many library APIs require headers — API keys, authentication tokens, content types.

Use `http.NewRequest` to build a request with custom headers before sending it:

```
req, _ := http.NewRequest("GET", url, nil)
req.Header.Set("X-API-Key", apiKey)
client := &http.Client{}
resp, err := client.Do(req)
```

Add a second header `X-Library-ID` with value `"lib-42"` to the request.

codeChecks.message: Set the X-Library-ID header: req.Header.Set(\"X-Library-ID\", \"lib-42\")
Replace the TODO comment with your header.

successMessage: Headers set, headers read. API keys, library IDs, authentication tokens — all follow the same pattern. r.Header.Get on the server, req.Header.Set on the client.

title: Build a mini library REST API
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a mini REST API for a library catalog:

1. Define a `Book` struct with `ISBN` string, `Title` string, `Author` string, `Available` bool (all JSON tagged)
2. Create a `[]Book` slice with 2 initial books
3. Write a `listHandler` that responds with all books as JSON
4. Write an `addHandler` that reads a JSON body, appends a new Book, and responds with 201
5. Route `GET /books` → listHandler, `POST /books` → addHandler (check `r.Method`)
6. Test: GET first, then POST a new book, then GET again — the catalog should grow

Expected final GET output includes all 3 books as JSON.
hint: Use json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(books) to respond. Use json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&newBook) to read the body.

codeChecks.message: Define a Book struct.
Add JSON tags to the Book struct fields.
Define a listHandler function.
Define an addHandler function.
Check r.Method to route GET vs POST.
Use json.NewEncoder(w).Encode to respond with JSON.
Use json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode to read the POST body.
Return 201 Created for successful POST.

successMessage: A working library REST API — list books, add books, check availability. GET returns JSON, POST reads JSON and appends, the catalog grows. This is how every Go library API is structured in production. You just built one.
